tier1.title: Conceptual Foundation
tier1.content: Electric forces act between charged objects. Like charges repel; opposite charges attract. What charges are involved, and how are they arranged? Are you finding a force on one charge due to others?

tier2.title: Relevant Principles
tier2.content: Coulomb's law: F = k|q₁q₂|/r², where k ≈ 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C². Force is along the line connecting charges. Superposition: net force on a charge is the vector sum of forces from each other charge individually. Electron charge: e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.

tier3.title: Targeted Guidance
tier3.content: Draw a diagram with charge positions. For each pair, compute F = kq₁q₂/r² and draw the force vector on the charge of interest (direction: away if same sign, toward if opposite). Resolve into x and y components if needed, then sum.

tier4.title: Near-Solution Scaffold
tier4.content: Calculate distance r between charges. Apply F = k|q₁q₂|/r² for each interaction. Assign signs to components based on direction. F_net,x = ΣF_x, F_net,y = ΣF_y. Magnitude: |F| = √(F_x² + F_y²). Direction: θ = tan⁻¹(F_y/F_x).

tier5.title: Full Solution Placeholder
tier5.content: Tier 5 is generated by the AI tutor with a complete solution and reasoning comparison.

tier1.title: Conceptual Foundation
tier1.content: Electrostatics deals with stationary charges. Which charge feels the force you're solving for? How many other charges contribute to the net electric force on it?

tier2.title: Relevant Principles
tier2.content: Charge is quantized: q = ne. In conductors, excess charge resides on surface. Insulators can hold charge throughout. Force decreases with 1/r² — doubling distance reduces force by factor of 4.

tier3.title: Targeted Guidance
tier3.content: For three or more charges, compute pairwise forces one at a time — don't add charges before calculating force. Use symmetry when possible: equal and symmetric charges may cancel components. Watch units: charge in Coulombs, distance in meters.

tier4.title: Near-Solution Scaffold
tier4.content: Convert all charges to Coulombs. Compute each F = kq₁q₂/r². Decompose into components with correct signs. Sum components. Convert microcoulombs: 1 μC = 10⁻⁶ C. Verify force is in Newtons and direction makes sense (attraction vs repulsion).

tier5.title: Full Solution Placeholder
tier5.content: Tier 5 is generated by the AI tutor with a complete solution and reasoning comparison.
